speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was, whether it's changed more here or in Norway, where you came from in the last 50 years?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh,

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: it's changing there too. It's following, following up what changes we had here. The changes come here first and then they follow back there. That's about it. But they've changed an awful lot in the last, well, I was over there in 1948 and then I was over there again last summer and it's changed an awful lot. And those years, 40, 50, 68, that'd be 27 years, if attached, you know, don't seem like that long. But it's really changed a lot then in that time. Well, at that time there wasn't hardly any private owned automobile. They had buses, you know, passenger buses at people, transported people, you know, from around the country and into towns and shopping places. And now putting everybody got their own automobile, same as we have here. Another thing, you know, they didn't have any homes, they didn't have any refrigeration, like automatic washers and deep freeze and refrigerators and all that. They didn't have them back there then in 48. Well, there might have been a few here and there maybe, but very few among the people in the country, you know. Now they all got them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there fewer people farming, fewer people in the country now than there were?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Not many have left the farms going to the country. There's a lot of them working now. They have jobs, but still they stay in the country and they go to town. They have regular buses at all the laborers, labor man, they got the buses there so most town, you know. Of course, it's different terrain there, you know. Most of the country there is valleys, you know. People live in the valleys and then there's mountains on each side. Well, they got a main road up through that valley and the valleys are not wide or narrow. So then you got that main road there and everybody come into that, you know, the buses go on that main road so you can leave your home there in the morning if you're going to work or even going to school. You take that bus and then it brings you back in the evening, you know. School kids ride on them too. Which is a real, that's a real good arrangement they have there. Good transportation for people, you know. But now, getting life here, you know, they're on their own cars. Maybe the bus will go there and still they go in their car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what do you think, change more here since, in the last 25 years or there? Or can you compare?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, I think it actually changed a little more over there in the last 25 years than it has here. The changes here come along sooner or earlier, you know. I mean, well, you know, like automobiles, refrigeration, modern homes and all that, they came here, you know, a long time ago. And they didn't have it over there. They've got this, you know, in the last 25, 30 years.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I want to ask you about old threat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what you call it, the law of inheritance? Oh, the old little threat?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, you didn't explain that to me really before.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, the old little threat, you know, that is, if you have a farm back there, why, the oldest boy has to write to the farm ahead of the other children. That's what they call an old little threat. If he owns the farm, well, he gets it and the others can't get it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's a law?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: That's a law. It's an old English law. Or if there's no boys, then the oldest girl has the old little threat, you know. She can claim the farm ahead of the other kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the father and mother don't have the right, if they wanted to give it to the second kid, they couldn't?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: If they did, the oldest boy, if he wanted, he could come in and take it away from them. The way they do that, it happens once in a while, and well, maybe at one time, maybe none of the kids want the farm, you know. They don't want the farm, so maybe it's sold, some outsider, you know, which don't happen very often back there. Well, then later on, if this oldest boy there decided, oh, I made a mistake, I should have held on to my farm, well, he can actually go back and claim it. The way they do that, they get three appraisals, and they appraise that farm, and then this boy with the oldest threat to that farm wants it, well, he got to pay that price, and he can take the farm away from that other guy. It happens once in a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can the farm be divided up into pieces, or does it have to all stay in one piece?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, they do divide them up sometimes, quite a bit.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Like if they have a pretty good -sized farm, maybe they'll, and there's a couple of boys like to farm both, or maybe sometimes they divide them.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: They agree, they make an agreement. Yeah, they make an agreement, they divide them.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Most of that culture back there isn't actually,

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: you know, land there isn't sold that you can make a big farm, and stuff like that, it's kind of taxes, you know, small operations. That'll say, around there where I came from, in the western part of Norway, you get over on the eastern part next to Sweden, then you get into bigger farms, you know, more open country where they have bigger farms. But the western part of Norway, all along the coastal area there, like small farms and valleys, lots of mountains, you know, them fjords goes way back inland, you know, and then they have a little patch here and there that they clear, you know, people live on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did the oldest rat have much to do with why people would leave Norway and come to America, because they couldn't get a farm?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, I really don't know, it has much to do with that at work.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I wanted to ask you about the history in school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you went to school, did you study history of Norway? Did they teach you?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, we had history. Oh yeah, we had history of the world and all.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, we actually had pretty good schools back there, even when I went to school there. Yeah, we had history and we had Bible history and catechism, we had religion taught in school there, you know, every day, you know. And we had lessons we had to learn by heart when we came to school, everyone has to stand up and recite our lessons and catechism and Bible history.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, I was thinking the history of Norway goes back so far compared to the history of this country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you studied that when you were a kid, you must have been teaching you way back around the year 1000 or something?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, oh yeah, the year 1000, yes.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Like when the religion came into Norway, that was about the year 1000. They had a king there, he was religious, you know, they called him Olot and Helige. Holy, Olot was his name and Helige was holy I guess. They called him Olot and Helige. He was the one that brought religion into Norway really. He, for some way or another, he was over in England quite a lot, you know, then he came back to Norway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the religion before it was Christianity?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, that I really, well they believed in like Thor, you know, they thought I was a god like Tundra, lightning, Thor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the Norsemen?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they from Norway?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, they called it Tor, you know, they believed there was a god up there, you know. I've seen pictures of Thor, you know, that caused the Tundra and lightning.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I guess they had some, I really, really don't remember too much about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was everybody Norwegian in the area where you lived or were there other cultural groups too? Was everybody Norwegian?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh everybody was Norwegians, yeah, them days pretty much yet.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, maybe in the cities you get a few outside countries, you know, people come in but in the country, you know, they're all... Well, once in a while there may be a trade or a day, a pin, you know, something like that. But pretty much straight Norwegian race.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, one thing I was wondering about was the difference between Norwegians and Swedish peoples, cultural differences, are they about the same or is there much difference between the two peoples?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, it's about the same.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I'd say about the same, yeah. Yeah. And languages, there's quite a bit of life, so everybody can make out, you know. It's just a difference in the language but I can understand Swedish and Danish.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And, well, I don't know. The trouble there, you know, in the early days they always had wars among themselves, you know, Swedes, Norwegians and the Danes, you know. So they hated each other, you know, because it was wars. Didn't get along enough, you know. Well, like even here in Troy, you know, when I came here they had two churches. They had a Norwegian Lutheran and a Swedish Lutheran. They didn't get along enough to go to the same church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the communities, the Norwegian and Swedish communities? Did they get together very much or did they stick to themselves?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No, I think maybe along the borders there, you know, people, you know, and back and forth there, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't most of social life around the church? Most of it?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, that's right. Yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, when I was a kid back there we had what they called Oomdom's Log, you know, that was a league for young people, you know. We had meetings, you know, doings, as you know. And then we had another organization, the Temperance Organization, you know, that put a strong back there when I left, you know. I belonged to that, but, you know, Temperance, you know, it wasn't allowed to taste any liquor or anything, you know. It was quite an organization. We had a lot of activities and that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about here? Were most of the Swedish and Norwegian people pretty religious here?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, yeah, used to be quite a bit, yeah. Everybody went to church and belonged to the church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were Nick and John?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, they went to church every Sunday. Yeah, they were really pretty strict on that, you know. I wouldn't say that they were really so religious, but they went to church every Sunday.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So you probably went too when you were... Oh, they insisted that I went, yeah. Well, they used to have a Methodist church down here, you know, below Clams, there, Les Clams, you know, he lives down in the highway, you know. Right below there, down at the corner in the flat there, they used to have a Methodist church. And that was kind of a neighborhood church here. Most of the time around there were Methodists there, you know. And everybody went down there and had Methodist church. So I started going down there too, you know, because I got to know somebody, younger people around here and what they know. Nick and John, they didn't think I should do that. They said you better stay with the Lutheran church, you know. They didn't think I should go down there. But I used to go down, we used to have choir, I joined the choir down there and we used to have choir practice sometimes in the evenings. And I used to walk right across the field there down, it's just a little ways across the field there, you know. Down to church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, where did you have to go to get to the Lutheran church? To Troy?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: To Troy, yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I was in Troy. It used to sit right there by Frank Brockey's house, where he built that greenhouse there now. It was a Lutheran church set when I came here. The Norwegian Lutheran church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there holidays that were Norwegian holidays that were any different from the Americans?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, well, at Christmas time we used to have three holidays back there and Easter you had three holidays.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: What is it, Friday, Good Friday and Thursday, both. Thursday, Good Friday, that was two holidays that we had back there. It was a good time when they first came over here, when it kind of kept them holidays for a while and finally kind of drifted away, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When the kids were having entertainment, did they dance or have games they played?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No, they played games.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No, the parents around here, they didn't believe in dancing, you know. I thought that was a sin, you know, to dance, but we played games, which I thought was alright.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of games would you do?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, like Skip to My Lou and...

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And...

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Three Deep and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What? Three Deep for your band, yeah? No, what's that?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And the last couple of hours, you know, and stuff like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you form a Three Deep, boy, you never played that?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, that was a game, you formed a circle, you know. So you have this two on, one behind the other, standing all around the circle. Well, then there was one, you know, there was a tag, and then there was one, three, there was three, you see. Well, then there's this one that was trying to catch the third fellow, you tagged him, you know. He was trying to catch him. If there was three there, you run over there and try to... ...pat the one behind, the third one behind on the back, see, and then he was it then. And he had, he would be the one that tagged around, try to catch the other one. So... They called it Three Deep.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So there's only one person was it, right?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, yeah. And he could pick any of the two and try to get to the one behind. Yeah, uh -huh.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: That's it. That sounded like it could be a lot of fun. It was kind of a fun, good game, yeah. Well, in the last couple outs, you played that, I'm sure, you know. I know, no, this is before my time. This is the last couple out. Well, that was really an outside game, though, but it was played a lot. Uh... You, um... There'd be a boy and a girl, you know, you'd be lined up two and two, boys and girls, right behind the other one. Well, then there was one here up in front was trying to catch the last couple out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had to run out, see?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: One on each side and come up. And then this guy was trying to catch one of them, then... Then he got this partner and would step up in front, you see. Well, then there's another guy, then there's the last couple behind again that had to run out, you know. He just kept on that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was always the last couple that had to run?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, it was the last couple, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And all he had to do was catch either one of them?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And then the other guy had to end that pair, one of them be alone, see. He'd catch one of those pairs or one of them, then that'd be alone, so he was the one that had to catch the next.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: That's funny, I've never played any game.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you? You never heard that?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No, I've heard of Skip to My Lou, but then that's probably a song. Yeah, there was a song with that one, you know.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Skip, Skip, Skip to My Lou, my darling.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, yeah, there was... Oh, we played them back in the old country too, done games.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Same games?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, same games, yeah. We had them back then too. Like Three Deep, the last couple out, and...

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I don't know, it was a few more, there was quite a lot of games.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the kids have much time to spend together in this country? Kids? Yeah, well, did you have a lot of time or was it all work and not much play?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, it was Sunday, you know, holidays. Saturday nights we generally had, well, we had literary. Only in the schoolhouse here, there wasn't time, and we had plays. Plays too. Yeah, we had a lot of plays. I didn't know that. Oh, yeah. And the littler, and... Yeah, we even... Yeah, I was in the publishing place too, quite a bit. Around here, and we showed them in the schoolhouse on Saturday nights.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they be in English or in Norwegian?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, yeah, in English.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I used to get, well, I suppose I was kind of thought broken. Most of the time they had some place that was parched by some, some like an Irish or something, kind of a little different, broke, you know. And they generally gave them to me.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I wonder where the plays came from.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You remember what kind they were?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No, I really don't.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: That sounds interesting.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: It sounds like it was all entertaining yourself, and nowadays it's the opposite. We entertained ourselves, and we had home entertainments, you know. We never went to town, and they used to be unmeant for taking anything in town. Sometimes I'd look up here. Some letters you had parties, you know, house parties. You'd go off and party from house to house. You know, at a time, we had a lot of that. Some letters.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would a house party be? They'd give eats and stuff like that?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, they'd have lunch, and they'd play games. Maybe sometimes we'd play, play within cards was another thing, you know, they didn't believe us. We had other card games, you know, besides the regular playing cards. We'd play sometimes, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With special kind of cards?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, games with special kinds of cards, yeah. No, they didn't believe in the regular playing cards we'd play.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Said too good with a lot of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you use town for? What people do when they go to Troy?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, shopping mostly. You know, everybody in them days would go to town on Saturdays, you know. All the farmers would go to town on Saturdays, and there'd be pretty big crowds in town on Saturdays. Because that's when everybody managed to go to town, go in with their horses. Maybe they took in eggs, you know, they had cream cans, butter and stuff like that. They always had something to sell them days, you know, the farmers. And they'd bring back groceries. A lot of them, you know, kind of broke even on that. They'd take in enough cream and eggs and stuff to pay for the groceries.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Everybody had chickens, you know, in them days. A few milk cows, everybody would milk a few cows. Some of them would try to sell the cream and others maybe sell the butter. The stores would trade them for groceries.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of groceries would people buy? What would they need if they couldn't grow on their groceries?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, they didn't buy much them days. Sugar and coffee and flour, everybody backed their own brand. They didn't actually buy much in the grocery stores them days. Everybody put up their own meat. I remember every used to butcher, most of the farmers would butcher maybe four or five, six hogs in the fall. And they'd cure them, sugar cure them or put in brine and smoke it and they would keep. They used to can a lot of beef, you know, they'd butcher them, maybe they'd beef them. Can most of that. And like Hamburg they ground a lot of that and they used to put it down in big stone jars and then we ran to some lard on the top to cover it and it would keep. Of course they'd salt it with pepper and, you know, cure it with salt and stuff. It would keep. Long time. That canned meat, I really liked that and I still like that. Pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would they do that together? Would they have the butcher bees?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, they used to kind of double up in the dungeon.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And then they canned a lot of fruits and stuff too, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the merchandise stores in Troy?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Your general stores too, you could buy, it must have been.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, they had one there, it was Anderson's grocery store and then Tom Christie had a grocery store in Troy when I came here. And then Butcher Johnson, he had a butcher shop in Troy when I came here.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I've heard about him. He's a funny guy. Yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And then a little later, you know, they got, well, Olson & Johnson were there too when I came. There was a kind of a department store. They had groceries and they had clothes and they had hardware. And that store was the Johnson store. Big store, that's a school bus garage now. That was a big store when I came here. That's the first store I stepped into when I came to Troy. The Johnson store, I remember that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that where you were looking for a way out here?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, yeah. I miss Alfred Sondel, you know, he's the one that met him by the depot there. He took me up in town, he was kind of looking for somebody from out here, you know. Maybe I get a ride out here, see. And he found Mushless there, so I got a ride up at Mushless in the hack out here. He took me right up here to Nicky John.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So people would get, probably they socialized a lot in Troy when they were there on Saturday.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, that was the meeting days, you know, everybody would sit on the street and they'd talk. Most of the language, there were Scandinavian languages on Saturdays down on the street when I came here. Yeah. And that's it, you know, that's the reason why everybody went to town on Saturday, then everybody meet. Only talk and listen. Do a little shopping.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: What about places to sit down inside, like they got DDs today. Well, they had two pool halls there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Where they could play pool and where they served a little lunch and soft drinks. Christy was one of them, Christy Brothers. That's where the Troy Tavern is now. They didn't have no liquor there in Troy when I came here. No beer or no liquor. But they had two pool hall, open places, you go in there and you could buy a cup of coffee, a little lunch, or you could sit around and play pool or play cards. They had card tables in there. And that's where we, a lot of times, you know, it was cold out and we'd go in there, you know, sit down and then have a cup of coffee, a little lunch or something. Then they had the hotel there and the corner were enormous now, that was the hotel, restaurant there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have movies when you came?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, part of the time, not all the time, but they had movies there when I came. The movie was across the street, up in there, in that area where the clinic is now, or Floyd's server station. In that area there was some movies there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they very popular then?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, quite popular, yeah. Then they had a bowling alley there for a while too, but that didn't last long, just for a short time there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But Troy was actually more active than it is now, you know what I mean, there was more business places than you have now. There were three grocery stores and I only had one.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Of course now they got the beer parlors.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: They'd stay in the hotel, maybe all night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They salesmen?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, and all them would travel on the train between the towns, they'd probably stay in town overnight.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Then they had to put a good rest, didn't they?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No salesman anymore to speak of like they used to be I guess.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well they still have all that, but they don't stay very long, they'd come in their car, you know. Come in town for an hour and they'd go on. And before you come in town on the train, you had to wait until the next day to get to the next town on the train. Couldn't take off when you wanted to, but you had to take off on the train with that. Made a lot of difference.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I bet the depot is a pretty busy place. Oh it was, it was.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: A lot of people traveling on the pass on the train and stuff. Well, that was about the only way to get around, you know, was to train. You go on the Gorse and place, why, if you're on the train, you have no buses. Like the Spokane, you see on the walk -on, they'd pass on the trains they had. I think they had two, I mean, there used to be one come in there around noon, then the other one about five o 'clock. I forgot whether you could go up the Spokane back to say today or what, I don't think you could. I think you went to Spokane, you had to stay overnight to come back the next day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you been on that train down to Lewiston?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: People don't ride on that anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a nice trip through the canyon there, going down the canyon?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, it was a pretty nice trip.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, I've been on the train lots of times through Lewiston and Spokane. In fact, I've ridden quite a lot on the train, you know, different places. The course, I used to go down the course quite often. On the train.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I wanted to ask you about those farming years when you were really farming. You told me that in the first couple of years there you worked out, and then after that you started renting.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the first place you rented was near Clems?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, the first place I rented was that 40 acres that Lester Clems lives on. There was an old house down there to just about where Lester Clems' house sits now that I stayed and lived there. I lived there for, I don't know, one year, two years I guess when I farmed that. That was the first farm I done. I mowed it was in the 40 there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what did it take for you to farm that place, to put in a crop and harvest it in those days? What did you need and how did you do it?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, what I done there, I bought the team of horses and a walking plow and bought a couple of sections of harrows. I put that 40 all in beans the first year. But I hired the bean planter. I didn't have a bean planter, so I hired a guy over there named George Strome. And I hired him to plant my beans there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I remember, you know, he had a, I had a team that didn't match the yellow kind of a bean there and then the younger coat kind of a workhorse. So George, he wanted to trade horses with me, you know. He thought I should, he had one, the bay one just like the one I had and said, you make a nice match team, you better trade me that other one for my bay and you have a nice match team. So I ended up, I traded with him. And by the time he got through planting the beans. But I, well I had $500 saved up, I remember that when I started farming and then I bought them horses and I had to buy some hay and I had to buy, I bought them in the fall of the year. And I plowed that with that foot burner that fall. And then I bought some oats and then I had to buy some seeds, you know, next spring, beans, seeds. And then of course I had to live, you know. But I,

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I, the Olds and the Johnsons down there, they agreed to carry me, you know, the groceries from the spring till in the fall. Like the cell, you know, have the beans harvested. So I got, I got the groceries on time. But I, I remember that by the time next fall, by harvest time, the next fall when my $500 was all gone, I didn't have any money left.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But when I sold the beans there, you know, I, I got my $500 back, so I had them back then. I might have sold a little bit more, well, there was expenses, but I remember that I had got enough out of that bean crop till, so I had my $500 back. Which wasn't too bad, see. No, from one year, that sounds good. No, because I worked out too a little in harvesting, which we used to trade work to, you know, quite a bit foundation, like in thrashing. One guy helped the other one, the exchequer worked back and forth to hold down the overhead to hire him. So what I've done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then what happened after that?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, in the next year, then I, I farmed that, and then I got some more land from Nick back in there, you know. Oh, he didn't let me have a field back that I put in beans too.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So, so that, that went pretty good. And then this was in, well, about 1915, 1916 in there, you know.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And, and that next fall the bean prices went up quite a bit. That was about the time that the First World War started over in Europe, you know. So the prices on our farm products here went up quite a little. So I, I don't remember exactly, but I know I done pretty good, I done quite a bit better, I feel. Better than if I had worked for wages, you know, quite a bit better. Well, I helped Nick do up here the work to quite a bit for him, and then, then he let me use some of his horses, you know, once in a while. So I actually did buy more horses. We, you know, exchanged, I worked for him a few days, and then I get to use his horses there, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you keep expanding from then?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, yeah, I ran a little more land, and I got a little more land from Nick here, and then, then there was a fellow named Walker over here. I ran some from him there one year, and so, farmed a little bigger, and done a little better. Farming was pretty good, you know, then, for then a few years, and then, well, say up to about 19, 18, 19 in there, you know. And ran from 16 up to 19, 19, 19, it was pretty good farming, good prices. Beans there won't fall when up to about 11, 12 cents, you know, which was unheard of. Weaned us up about two and a half, you know, which was pretty good prices that day, so. So it was pretty good, but then about 19, 20, then, then we got the crush there, you know. When the bottom went out, or the prices, you know, beans down to three cents, wheat down to a dollar, and it just tumbled right down. And from then on, you know, it was pretty tough going and farming, mainly for many, many years. Then you got in the 30s, then you got still working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened all of a sudden? Was it the end of the war?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: End of the war.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah. Maybe, well, maybe overproduction, I suppose, the high prices were high, and everybody tried to grow more, you know, natural. And then the war ended, and then we lost foreign markets, you know, and prices just tumbled. Well, I remember, I took a trip back to Norway in 19, 20, and I remember coming back, I came back here in the fall, I went over there in May, and I came back here in November. And I remember coming on the train across, you know, wheat was dropping, it was up to two and a half. And by the time I got to Troy, it was a dollar. I remember that, seeing the paper every day, it would drop.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Beans was down to, I think, from three and a half cents.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many people get hurt around here?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, a huge, yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, I know some guys that had 168, had it paid for, and then they wanted to get big, and then they buy small land, and they mortgage everything they had to buy this land. Lost it all. Some guys, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you decided to come back here before the crash, right?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, yeah, yeah. The crash just started about the time that I was on the way over here. Yeah, yeah, I had, I wrote to Nick, Nick Olson, you know, and asked him for thinking about coming back, if I could rent some land, you know, I didn't, and otherwise I didn't know whether to come back or not, I might not, I might have stayed over there. So then he told me I could have the lower place done, he had another place down in the canyon down there, you know, which is a really good farm, you know, lost pasture down the canyon. Yeah, I said if you come back, I'll rent you that lower place down there. Well, that sounds pretty good, you know, I figured I could really do pretty good down there. So I got ready to come back right away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was that your thinking, you wanted to have a better prospect to have more land if you were going to come back?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, that's right, yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, my intentions was strictly farming, you know, that's what I had to do, come back, and of course when he said I could have that farm down there, well, I could have done pretty good if the prices would have stayed up, like we had them, but it changed quick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do when you got back?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, I went to work down there, you know, and I don't know what, if I bought a few cattle and a few hogs or anything, anyhow, you know, I got ready to, you know, farm. Started getting some horses, you know, and hay and stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a hard time then because of the prices?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, I don't know really such a hard time, but I never got any worse, I never gained any, but that was about it. Just break even maybe a little bit better, that's about all. But then I bought a trash machine, you know, a little bit later there, and I customed trash around here for, you know, a lot of people around, I used to trash around this whole neighborhood for about, oh I think 16 years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which year did you start?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, it was later, it was probably back in 25, so I bought a trash machine. Well, I had half interest in one, maybe a little earlier than that, maybe 24. I bought out a guy, Albert Olds, and him and his brother Bob, they were partners, and I worked for them at harvest, and oiled, separated, kind of worked around the separator, you know, oiling and one thing or another. Well, then the father says, you better buy me out, you said I'd like to quit, and he says you know how to run this machine now, he says you've been around here this harvest. So it ended up I bought his half, so then Bob and I, we were partners. So we trashed there for a couple of years I think or so, maybe more, I can remember for sure. And then I sold out to Bob, and then I bought a small outfit, a gas outfit, this was big steam outfits, you know, we had a big steamer, big machine, cookhouse, we fed all the men and all that. But that went pretty good, we done pretty good trashing already, made a little money there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Carl Olson's brother?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: The gas outfit, yeah, I bought that from Carl Olson, yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: He told me about Ollie Bowman giving you the loan for that thing, he'd give it to you for that but not for Carl.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But there wasn't much competition around here then, or was it an open field for fresh?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, it was, yeah, there wasn't much competition here, in fact I was the first one in here with a small outfit, gas outfit, and they all liked them gas outfits, you know, that's one reason why I kind of went out of the big steam deal, you know, because everybody, you know, favored the smaller rigs in the gas outfit, for some reason or another.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there real advantages over steam to the small?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, yeah, I think so, a little advantage. You had smaller crews for one thing, and, well, with a steam rig you had a lot of horses, you had four horses on the water wagon, you had to haul water for the steamer, you know. And then you had a bigger crew, and then you had maybe three or four horses around the cookhouse to move the cookhouse with, and then you had about eight or ten bundle wagons, you had a lot of horses. Well, smaller farmers, they kind of hate to see you move in on them, they'd come in in the evening, you know, and they had to feed all them horses overnight and all that. And then they preferred the little ones, less outfits, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they compare for work done? Did the gas one put out near as much as steam?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, a little bit more per man, I think, yeah, or team than the big outfits. Yeah, I know I used to put out with that outfit, I had five bundle wagons, a haul in the bundles, and I used to have three pitchers. And then the big outfits here I had around five pitchers and maybe nine bundle teams, you know. Well, I used to do a little better per man with a small outfit. I used to try something, oh, 700 sacks a day, you know, which was pretty good. Well, with the big rig, we figured if we got 900 or 1 ,000, why, we'd do them. That's about the best we could do. So it was a little better.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you start in the season? What month did you start harvesting in?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, some years we'd get started, maybe the last of July, it was generally in August, around the first to tenth of August before we'd start threshing. But once in a while, they had an early week that they called 40 fold, and they'd seed that early in the fall, and sometimes they'd harvest that the last of July. So it depended on, you know, we'd try to start as soon as they'd think ready to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you usually have a certain part of this area you'd start with and work your way around, or did you have a set order you went in?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Not exactly, no.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No, we'd just thrash this and get ready, you know.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Of course, everybody was anxious when the grain was ready to thrash, to get it thrashed, you know, and we'd go around whatever. Of course, Kildee down here, he was the biggest farmer in here then, he farmed. I generally had about a week to ten days running on him alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, how much land did he have?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, he farmed quite a bit. I don't know, he must have farmed, well, four or five hundred acres. He used to farm at Stanford Place, that must have been over 200, and then Doc Wick Place was around three. Yeah, he farmed over five hundred acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about most of the farms? How big were they?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Almost about 160. Some less than that, some farmed only 80, maybe 100, 120.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had they cleared up most of the land on their places by then?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, pretty much.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: There hasn't been, this area here, it was pretty well cleared up when I came here. The only thing different here than now, they used to have big orchards here, big orchards. They pulled out, you know, like up there in that hill, that old cabin, that old hill up there was an orchard when I came here. Apples and prunes and pears and some cherries.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And the same thing down on the Troy Hill, you know, that field there, kind of along that new hive, down toward that hole, that was an orchard when I came here. Down here, old Jockie had a big orchard. Down on the Nix Lower Place down there, he had 20 acres of orchard down there, mostly prunes. There was one time here, a long, long time ago, that there was big money in fruit and everybody went for orchard, planted orchard, and then they overdone it. Couldn't sell any out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the harvesting? Did they have to, did it just down in the area, did people come in and harvest or what?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, no, it used to be people come in, you know, in the fall and pick the fruits, you know, pick fruit. Yeah, from up north, like Derry, Boville, Elk River area, used to come down here and pick fruits, you know. In the fall, they would save some, not such a great experience.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your harvest crew? Were they all local people or did many of them come in?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, there was quite a bit of them locally, yeah, most of them local. Well, there was a couple of years there during the, there was in the 30s when they had the dry area back in Montana and Dakota, when they all dried out back there. Then there was a lot of people come out here from back there looking for work, so I had some guys here from Montana and Dakota working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had the same crew year after year pretty much?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Most of the time I'd have about the same bundle of hoes, they were all local, I had about the same ones every year. For many years, I used to have Belton Cunha and Marvin Chaney and Rudy Anderson and Jerry Peterson and, and, uh, Clarence Steelesman. There was five Hall Buns for me for years and years. Abner Korka was another one. He used the Hall Buns for many, many years. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your cooking?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, we had the cook house there for quite a few years. You don't know Jenny Baughman down in Troy, the critical lady down there, she cooked for me. And Lawrence's sisters, two of them, we had two, we had two cooks, they cooked for me.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of that work? Was that good work, you think?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, that was pretty good. Everybody had a pretty good time in harvest, looked forward to it, they had a lot of fun, you know, crowd grew together. And everybody seemed to enjoy themselves in harvest. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sleep out most of the time?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Sleep in the straw stack, sure. Everybody had their bed rolled along, you know. Moved the cook house wherever it was at, and the cook house would follow and eat there and then sleep in the straw stack. Yeah, of course, if you got a rain, then you ought to have a hit for a barn some place, you know. Yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah. That's what they call the good old days, you know.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the money in it all pretty good?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, I actually done pretty good. Yeah. I done pretty good thrashing. I make money every year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About how did it break down? What was the, what was it going wages?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, about that time I think I paid the sack source.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I had two sack source. Oh, four or five dollars a day. That was pretty heavy work, you know, so on sacks. And there wasn't many guys that would do it, really. Well, the bottle haulers, the men, the team, I think when I first started they were getting four dollars a day. And I think the pictures I paid two and a half or three dollars a day.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And the cooks, I don't know what, I had two cooks. The first one probably was around four dollars and then the second was around two, two and a half, something like that. That was about it, start with it. And we worked twelve hours a day. That was the goal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And how long would the season be about?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, it worried some, but the longest I think I ever put in that we thrashed grain, then we got through with the grain thrashing, the thrashing beans, beans is always later. And there was one year we put in sixty days. But that was the longest. Ordinarily, you know, well, thirty days maybe. Thrashing, grain, and beans both, thirty -five.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember about how much you would clear after all your expenses on average year?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: That, well, I don't know. I used to, I think I used to pig around clearing around sixty dollars a day or something like that.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I know that first year when I bought that gas outfit. I did pretty good that year. I didn't quite pay for it, but fairly close. I got up there a couple of thousand dollars. I think I paid them, what was it, twenty, six hundred dollars I'll be able to get for that outfit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of shape were most of the farms in as far as how well off the farmer was in the late twenties when you started thrashing? Were they doing pretty good?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, I think they were fairly well off most of them, yeah. I think so. Oh, there were some in debt, you know, a little, but most of them were getting along pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Okay, well what happened when the depression started to be felt out here? About when was that?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, the real severe depression came in the thirties. Well, in the twenty nineties when you had the big crash, you had a kind of a crash there in the twenties, you know, after the First World War. It was pretty tough then, you know. Oh, not so real tough. You could make it, you know, I mean, you could make it all right and maybe realize a little, but the crash came in twenty -nine and in thirties, then it was impossible, really. Everybody really got kind of bad off, you know, and some of them let the land go off of taxes. They couldn't even come up with enough money to pay the taxes. They lengthened taxes them days for many years after that, like Timberlands, quit paying taxes. Even Potlach Corporation had a lot of land, they quit paying taxes on Timberland, you know. Money was really tight. Well, I tell you how bad farming was, like peas, there was a time you couldn't sell them at all. No market. And if you did get a buyer, maybe a dollar and a half, two dollars, a hundred, two cents a pound, wheat, I sold some wheat there for twenty -four cents a bushel. Well, you couldn't in the verse begin to grow for that, you know. And so it was really a squeeze. When everybody, they didn't have any money, everybody had to get along without money, you know, like, they had a flour mill down there by Colossac and we'd take wheat down there and trade for flour, you know. Well, maybe in the Genesee country they maybe needed wood for fuel, most of them burned wood down there for fuel, you know, for their cookstove and the heaters. Well, maybe they'd trade somebody up here north of town some pork or meat for cordwood. So people got started to trading, see, instead of buying or selling, nobody had the money to trade. If I had too much of something, I'd trade somebody else maybe for something else, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wait, would you say that again? You came when you got to Troy?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, I conducted on the railroad. He came and put his hands on my shoulder, tapped me on the shoulder and said, this is it.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And even off the train magazines walking around, he was trying to find somebody that could talk my language, I guess.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So, I found a guy named Alfred Sundell down there and Alfred, he took me up in town, he could talk, well he was Swedish really, but we made out all that. He told him where I was going and he said he could see if he could find somebody from out there. He went up in that Oldson Johnson store there and found Mushluts, he was in there with a team and a hat. Mushluts, he went right over in that house over there, the first house there, that's where he lived. So I got a ride out here with him and then right away. He took me right up there, with Nick and John Oldson there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You and your friend had split up by then?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, my friend, he got off in North Dakota. I think we separated them. Either Manhattan or St. Paul. He got another train there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: He stopped in North Dakota, he had some relatives there. Mm hm.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Since then he's moved to Minnesota. Last I heard him, he was up to Northern Park in Minnesota, close to the Canadian border.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you remember just when this was?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: This was 1913. I came to Troy the 22nd day of July, 1913.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Started pitching hay the next day.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But I remember that hour and a half he lost there. Yeah, it was awful hot that day too, I noticed that.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the country, what do you remember the country being like when you were first here? Do you remember what some of your first impressions of this country were? What you thought of it?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, I thought it was an awful big country, you know, for one thing. Mm hm.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Really,

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: really, what I thought of it, at first I thought it was awful hot here, you know, like in harvest here that year. Pretty hot. I know my face got sun burnt, my skin was peeling off my nose, all that. And, anyways, you know, as far as the land was concerned, I really thought this was good land here. They could raise good crops without fertilizing, just plow and hermit, you know, seed, get good crops. We couldn't do that back there, you had to have that natural fertilizer and everything. Otherwise you wouldn't grow everything much.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And the way they have big fields you had here, I really thought this was a big country and big fields. Everything big, like the trash in here, big trash machines, you know, trash. Thousand sacks a day, you know. That was really something.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, but they didn't have hardly any automobiles here. As far as I remember, there was only three automobiles in Troy when I came here.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And the roads, of course, they were awful dusty here in the summertime. All dirt, no gravel on the roads some days, when they drove with a wagon and a desk to fly, you know. All over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you see a big difference between this country and the country further east?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, of course, the hills here, you know, and the timber. Of course, when we came through the coders, well, we came through Minnesota there too, by the way. That's all prairie, you know, like in Minnesota, I remember there. We ran from there a long time there, you know, just on the open prairie there. Of course, when you get out here, you get into mountains and timber. And the rolling country, this is more like, really more like the old country out here, you know, than back east. Mountains and timber. That's what we had back there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But I really, I really thought this was really something here, the way it could produce.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And the people here were really, really nice and friendly too.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Everybody was just friendly. Remember, I started going down to that church, the metal church, you know. And it wasn't hardly a Sunday unless somebody would ask me out for dinner, go home for dinner or something. They were really nice. Yeah, really nice and friendly. This was an awful good neighborhood here. When I came here. Well, it is yet, as far as I'm concerned. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you get on with Nick and John?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, alright. Yeah, I stayed there for a long time, then I finally got to rent some of Nick's land.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I found, they had a hill that plays way down, about three miles down the canyon down there, and I found that for fifteen years. Well, I first started farming up here a little bit.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah. Well, they were pretty good guys, you know. I was just nothing but a kid, you know, and they put up with me, I guess.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And I think about it now, you know, the way I start out, you know, just seventeen years old, just a kid, you know, I can hardly believe it. You know, tackle.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Tackle, going away from home that young.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But I was stubborn, I was bound to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your parents try to talk you out of it?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh my, yes. Oh yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: They really did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about learning English?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, I don't know, it's a first or a second winter, I was here, why, go ahead. Why,

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Nick and John, of course we talked Norwegian up there pretty much, and they said you'll never learn English around here, they said you gotta get out someplace where they talk, it says, otherwise you'll never learn the English, you know. So then one winter I went over here, a fellow named Cunningham just lived over there just a little ways, he had a farm over there, he had two or three kids, he was a retired school teacher, a boat gun, they were boat school teachers.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And Nick and I think Nick had talked to him something about it, and Cunningham said, well come over here, just one of you, you worked a little. He's bored, we gave him his board, and stayed here and worked a little bit. Cutting some wood and got a few cows and chores. So I went over there and then I went to school there for about, I don't know, after Christmas anyhow, I think it was about six weeks, I think that was on the school I had after Christmas, about six weeks. Didn't have the long terms they got now. So I went to school there then, from Christmas until the end of the term, and I stayed there. I learned quite a bit of English that winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You learned at the Cunningham's house?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, well, going to school and Cunningham's house, and he had a boy you know, he was about eight, ten years old, and he was always talking to me, you know, and I probably learned more from him than anybody else. Of course, I went out and he'd say, well he'd always be with me and he'd be talking, and I felt more free around him, you know, too, to talk. So I learned a lot from him.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So that spring I was getting so then I could understand everything. I couldn't talk, but I could understand everything that people told me or talked to me about, you know. And then it gradually comes, if you're around where you're here, the last wave gradually comes. Of course I really never studied English language much, I should have, I should have studied more and so I felt like it so we could talk more correctly, I guess, but I just want to pick up from other guys.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: You talk is fine, I think if you tried learning it in the book it would be worse.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I wouldn't even know that, hardly, that you hadn't been born and raised here because there were a lot of people, you know, who were raised speaking Norwegian or Swedish and then when they started school they learned and you talked just as well as anybody.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No, I don't use good grammar many times. I notice it myself when I listen to somebody else how they say it, you know, my grammar isn't good. Yeah, I don't know about that.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But it must be unusual when you were in school in the winter because you must have been about the oldest or one of the oldest. Oh yeah, I was way the oldest in the school, yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I started in in the first grade, trout, they lived in the corner there in that house there and he had, boy they just started school that year, you know, so him and I started together. But I picked it up pretty fast. I got into the sixth grade, you know, before I got through that. Of course the arithmetic and a lot of stuff already had, you know, it was the language, the spelling was the most difficult to learn, still is. I had a terrible time. To write and to spell correctly. That takes a lot of studying, you know, to spell right. But you could probably read fine. Oh fine, yeah, I get a long fine reading.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So you got to meet a lot of the local kids when you were in school.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, oh yeah, after all the kids. Oh I got along good in school. We had the snowball fights and everything. Now I want to be in my side because I got a throat a little harder than the rest of us.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, I remember that. Oh I'll be on your side.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: He's probably as big as the teacher.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: He's probably as big as the teacher. Oh yeah, that's the bar again. Yeah, it was, her name was Mohenro. He's Mohenro, was a girl teacher.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, yeah, all the neighborhood kids went to school there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this the first winter that you were here?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I'm not sure it was the first or the second. I believe it was the first, I believe it was the first winter. Yeah, I'm pretty sure it was the first winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how did it work with Nick and John? Did they board you and you worked for them? How did that work? How long did that go on?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well I worked from off and on, not steady since I started farming. I started farming then. Third year I was here. But I didn't work for them steady but I kind of made that my headquarters there, you know. But I worked for the neighbors around here. But Nick had a lot of livestock, you know, horses and cattle. So in the wintertime there, why, they had to feed all them horses and cattle and haul and maneuver. I remember that one winter I hauled and maneuvered there, I don't know. Over a month I covered a whole field out there with the maneuver there, one with it all down, from all them horses. We had, well, we had a lot of piles that were piled up there, trying to get it all cleaned out once. Pitched it down, you know, hauled on a sleigh, team of horses.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And then they would sometimes haul the grain in from the trash machine, haul it in buildings. And then haul it to town on the sled in the winter. It wasn't so much nicer to haul on the sled than on the wagon. So some winters we'd haul grain for a couple of three weeks, you know, on sleds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just in to Troy?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah. Just piled a lot of grain in there, hauled it right in from the trash machine, and piled it in the memories. And that stairways, out of sacks, you just step sacks and stuff like that, and get way up high and pile it up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that just, they were living in the one or two room cabin then?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: They hadn't built the house then. The house there was built in 1920. I remember that for sure, because that's the year I went back to Norway.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So it was probably pretty primitive then, with just a wood stove and not much in the place at all.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No, no, not much. Very little. Just the stove, they had a cook stove, maybe a heater.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And a few chairs and tables. Didn't have much luxury them days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have a bedroll or something like that for your place to sleep?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah. Then they said, well, when we went and harvested, we slept out. You know, they had a cookhouse along with a machine, a trash, and we'd just move from one place to the other, and we'd sleep in a straw stack. Everyone had their bedroll, sleeping. So we'd just make our bed in the straw pile in the evenings. That's the way we lived and harvested. That was fine too, as long as the weather was good. But if it got to rain tonight, then we all had to get up and run for the barn, so someplace could get under the roof.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was the first farming that you did of your own, instead of just working out for other people?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, the first land I ranted was at 40 down there where Lester Clam lives. I grew beans then. I put it all in beans.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And that's the way we growed a lot of beans around there them days. We didn't grow any peas. We used to have peas, all beans, and wheat, and oats, and barley. Then they had their summer pollen beans, and then they put the bean ground into fall wheat in the fall. That was the rotation they had here then. Instead of now, I'd have peas and then put fall wheat on the pea ground. Some of them stick to summer pollen. So the first crop I growed was beans, white beans, white navy beans.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And we planted them in rows and colored them.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And we cut them with, they had a bean cutter with two knives that kind of went under the ground, you know, a little bit down under the ground, cut the beans off. And they come out in a row behind, and the knives got this much space behind. And then the knives come in and the beans kind of slide back on the knife and drop off the end back there. Then you'd chop them with a pitchfork, put them in little chops. And you'd trash them while you pitched them chops into the bundle wagons, you know, and then haul them in and trash them in stationary rigs.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So there was a lot of work to beans, but they were a pretty good crop for them days. And it was good ground for the fall wheat because you cultivated that ground in the summer and kept it clean for weeds, and then you had the moisture too by cultivating. So the wheat did real good on the bean ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was it you rented the place from?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Nick, no that was Ike Sligh, that first one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you have to, did you have money to go into the business at that time?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, I worked for two years there and I saved up $500. And that's what I went and bought my equipment, what little I bought, $500. I bought a team of horses and a wagon, some hares, I bought a little hay and some oats to feed for horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were your wages during those two years that you saved $500?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, I made between $300 and $400 a year, as I remember.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So I had a little spending money, and I didn't spend much.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I don't think so.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: That's what I had to, you know. But anyhow, out of the two years worked, I saved up $500 and I don't know how much the gross was, not over, oh I don't know, I'd save $700 to $800.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what the wages were for the work you were doing then?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, in harvest time we got two and a half a day, trashing, and for plowing and field work, seeding and harrowing and plowing, we got a dollar and a quarter a day. And in hay, I think we got two dollars a day, hay.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And then we got our board, place to stay with the people, the working men, they stayed at the place where they worked and they got their board.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So you'd sort of stay with Nick and John when you weren't working out and you'd go out and work for people. Yeah, sometimes I'd kind of come back there and stay there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: One year I went harvesting over in the Genesee country. I took a team and a wagon over there in all the tunnels. But that was after I started farming though. I could still after I started farming, I still went out and worked, you know, once in a while. If I can harvest the garden and harvest them. That was the main income of a lot of the working fellows around here them days, you know, was to go out and harvest. Get a good run and harvest and then save up enough money then to carry them over maybe next winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're saying there wasn't too much money in the farming itself then?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No, there really wasn't. But by the time I started, you know, things got better. You see, that was when the First World War started, you know, and the prices went up. Like the prices on beans went up, you know.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Quite a bit. So farming got better there for a few years. So it was pretty good if I thought I would have had the land then. It could have been pretty good. But I just had little patches, you know. So I didn't really make anything. But I did better than working for Vegas though. Of course I could still work out part time and then... So I come out better by farming a little and then worked out a little too. Then I thought I just worked out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was this first bean crop you planted, you remember?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: That was in... let's see, that was the third year I was here. No, 13 or 15, 16. That's it. That's the year I had beans over there in Ike's place in 1916.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you come out?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I came out pretty good. When I sold my beans that fall I got around $500. So I got my money back, you know, I put in. But I really spent all the five alright. I didn't have enough really to reach, go through that whole year and I got my bean crops. So I talked to Olson and Johnson there in the store, you know, if I could charge my groceries there till I got my beans harvested and they let me do that. So that was done a lot them days. It seemed like the store, they carried the farmers, you know, from maybe spring to fall till they got the harvest and then some crops sold.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you figure they would carry you?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, I didn't know I was a stranger and all that. I didn't know they would or not but I thought I'd ask them anyhow. They agreed to do it.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: This was the year of the first crop. Year of the first crop, yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So then it went on, went on, gained a little each year. Farming was pretty good after a few years and then later on I got tough again after, well after 1920.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: It was hard to make any money farming there for a long time. Really. After the first world war, prices really went down and it dropped.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But then they bought a trashing machine there. Later on. So I trashed around this country here, custom trashing. So I made a little money on that trashing machine.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I got enough money from the bank to buy that. That's really.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I was the old lady in Boulton when he was the president. He was the man that started the first bank of Troy. And he was the president and that was before Brodrick.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So I went down there and asked him if I could get some money to buy a trashing machine. And he said, you can stir it around a little bit. And he says, well, I think we'll let you have it. He says, you've got a chance to make some money. He says, with a trashing machine. But he says, if you'd come in here and I want you to get money to buy new cars or something, we'll let you have it. That's what I said.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: You had something important.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Something that could make something for you. Yeah, that's right.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: There's one thing about that bank that I never had to give a mortgage. I always got the money I needed on my note. I never asked for a mortgage on anything I haven't had. Oh, I don't know. Twelve, fifteen hundred. Trip bottoms, you know, modern. Well, I said, I bet you after the plough is done, you go out in the field. I said, I bet you can't tell where to use the new plough or use the old one. I did about the same job, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about land prices? When did they start going up from being just the way they were during the Depression?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, that's something gradually too, you might say.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Up till here, the last couple of years, you know, they've been rocketing, you know, the land prices, you know. Where a fella would probably sold his farm here about four years ago for three fifty, four hundred dollars an acre. Well now he's asking maybe seven hundred since the week. Went up to five dollars there once, you know, in peace a year ago, thirty cents. And of course the man that was farming big, why he made a lot of money there, those crops, you know, there. He's looking for more land when the prices up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the prices will stay up like this?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I doubt it. If the prices of the farm products go down again, I think land will sag down, slow up anyhow, a little.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what happened in the war, when the war came, World War II, then prices went up and things really got better again? Is that what ended the Depression here?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, that's when things got better again, you know.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: The farm got better again. I consider in there in the forties, say from 1940, 45, 48 in there, it's about the best times that farmer ever had when you consider the land prices and what they got for his produce off the farms. Why that was the best, best time they ever had. That was under Truman. Well that was the Second World War, you know, is what it was. Well land prices hadn't gone up then, you know, you could still buy pretty good land on a ridge, you set up a lot of land for around, oh, $75 an acre. Well at that time I remember peas, it was around 6 cents, and wheat was a dollar and a half, you know.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And it was easy then, you could buy land at $75 an acre and maybe pay for it in two or three years. Easy.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well then after that, you know, here land prices really never went back, it's gradually been going up, but the price of our peas and wheat and where we grow went way down, like peas went down below 3 cents.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was this?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, just about three years ago. Peas was 3 cents. And wheat, dollar and 30, 35 cents.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But land prices kept going up.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So it was pretty hard to make the thing for many, many years, after the Second World War too, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the prices controlled then, after the Second World War?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, they had a floor under wheat, nothing else, no peas or anything like that. But you had a loan price on wheat, that you could get a loan on it and get your money for the wheat and still have the wheat if it went up, you could sell it and pay off the loan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that loan price?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Dollar,

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: dollar and... I don't know. Oh, maybe it's less than that. Well then back there, probably less than that, maybe under a dollar, maybe 90 cents or something. But they had a parity price there, you know, for quite a while on farm product, which I figure was the fairest thing they ever had that should never have been taken off. That, the regulator support price on wheat according to what the farmer had to pay for machinery or fertilizer or all that. The machinery went up, fertilizer went up, the cost of production went up, then the support price of your commodities would follow that up, you know. And I figure that's the best deal that they ever had, that was a fair deal and that it should have been left on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was a floor at a minimum that you were guaranteed?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, it was a minimum, yeah, you was guaranteed a minimum there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this Truman, under Truman?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, they had under Truman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well then, after Truman, his administration, then one of them was coming in, Eisenhower I guess, wasn't it?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And Benson come in as the Secretary of Agriculture and I don't know anything about all that. Well that's when really the movement from the farm started going to town. They passed me the bench and he was kind of in favor of that. He said, you can't make any little farm, go and take a job and work for corporations. That was his attitude, he'd come right out there. Which was wrong, absolutely wrong. They should have tried to regulate the system so that the little farmers could have made a living on the farm instead of going to town. It was a wrong movement, a wrong thing. Now you got a great big labor force and unemployment. I've been wondering a long time if we wouldn't end up with a lot of unemployment because we're importing a lot of goods from other countries. The corporations are going over in other countries, building factories and make the stuff there and import it in here and then everybody has a business here. You got engineers sitting around figuring all the time to come up with a machine that'll take the place of the man. Why couldn't they have to run into unemployment?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there's any chance you could put people back on the small farms? Is there any possibility of doing that anymore?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well it depends. It's a pretty hard situation, all right. Of course there is young people now that's actually taking that away that they want to get out on a piece of land and make the living off the land. Which is what they've done in the old days. But to do that people have to change their way of living an awful lot. Of course now it takes a lot of money too, you know, not only the food, what you eat, but it takes a lot of money well for your cars and your clients, you know, refrigerators, sleep preachers, TVs and all this stuff that we didn't have in the old days. We didn't need much money in the old days. We could live off the land because we didn't need hardly any cash. But now it's different. That's a stick to the man. I think the man still got a small piece of ground out here and you can grow a lot of vegetables, you can grow fruit, you can grow nut meat and all that for eats to live. But you won't get any cash, you see, you won't have any cash. And your taxes are going up all the time, you know, so it takes quite a bit of cash now.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But people are willing to live like they did 60, 70 years ago and they can make it on a piece of ground to eat.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: It would be the old pioneer spirit coming back. Probably Nick and John and people like that would be glad to see it in a way.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, that's right. Well, I think people were actually more contented then when they are now just as happy as we are now, I believe. I actually believe it. People seem to have a pretty good time and enjoy themselves in the old days.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: It seems like now they need more and they get together less.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, that's it, you know, that's right. They get together less. Well, I don't know, it's a... Well, you take like Troy now, you go down. Troy, there you got how many places they got them in a cell booth. You got Burr, you got the two beer powders, that's three. Then you got Smith, that's four. Then you can sell some beer on the store, that's five. Then you go up to the restaurant, there are six. There are six places that sell liquor. A little place like Troy, only one grocery store.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Does that make sense?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I don't know. I think they should clean up the...

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: It's going the wrong way.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I just don't see, I wish I could see how you could go back because I like better what I know about the early days than what I know about the way things are going. But I just don't see how you turn it around, how you change it. There isn't too much power in the country because there aren't too many people.
